Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I'm juggling so much. I work part-time and have family responsibilities, so I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could—early mornings before everyone woke up, late nights, bus rides. I tried to dedicate about 17 hours a week, but it was rarely in neat blocks. I'd download readings and go through them during lunch breaks or waiting rooms. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what was happening at home. Some weeks I could maintain a somewhat regular schedule, but then a family emergency or extra work shifts would throw everything off. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd have intense study bursts. I'd sometimes go days with minimal studying, then cram in hours when something was due. It was exhausting but that's just my reality right now.
Q3: Usually a deadline would prompt me to log in—I'd realize an assignment was coming up and need to check requirements or submit something. Sometimes I'd get email notifications about new content or discussion posts and that would pull me in. Honestly, there were stretches where I forgot to log in because I was studying from downloaded materials. When I did log in, it was purposeful though—I didn't have time to just browse around.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every couple of days during my commute. It became almost automatic to open it on my phone and scan for updates. But then things got hectic with work and that habit fell apart. I never fully integrated it into my daily routine the way some students probably do—my life is just too unpredictable for that kind of consistency.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were absolutely essential for me—I learn best through text, so I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students asked questions I was too anxious to ask myself. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed written explanations alongside videos. Those text-based resources let me study offline, which was crucial given my limited data and unpredictable schedule.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally engaged with the video content because watching 250 minutes of video requires stable internet and uninterrupted time, which I rarely had. I'd sometimes let videos play while doing other things, which wasn't really learning. Some of the optional supplementary readings I ignored because I was just trying to survive the required material. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions—only twice—because I felt too anxious and behind to contribute meaningfully.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I needed to see the practical value immediately because I couldn't afford to waste precious study hours on things that felt like busywork. Clear instructions and obvious relevance to assignments made me prioritize something. If it felt vague or disconnected from my goals, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required activities got done because they had to, even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe if I have time' but that time never materialized. There was also this guilt around optional activities—like I should be doing them to be a 'good student' but I physically couldn't. Required activities had deadlines that forced action; optional ones just faded into the background.
Q9: Oh absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download readings, lecture notes, and any PDFs I could find, then study them on my phone or print them at the library when I could afford it. I used external YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't understand. I also made handwritten notes extensively—that's how I really process information. The LMS was more like a content delivery system than where my learning actually happened.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at the kitchen table with printed readings and handwritten notes, working through concepts for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. During my commute, I'd review materials on my phone from screenshots I'd taken. I formed a small study group with two classmates and we'd meet in person or text about coursework—none of that showed up anywhere. My real learning was largely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it immediately, and then never open it on the LMS again. I'd study that same document multiple times offline over the following weeks. This was partly about unreliable internet and partly about my preference for reading without being connected. The LMS would show one brief access, but that single download might represent ten hours of subsequent study.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My stress was through the roof, my anxiety was constant, and I felt completely overwhelmed. Work increased my hours, a family member got sick, and I just couldn't keep up with the 18 courses worth of content. I'd log in and feel this wave of dread seeing all the things I was behind on. The boredom crept in too—when you're exhausted and anxious, even interesting material feels like a chore. I had to pick and choose what to engage with just to survive.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those factors affected me constantly. Fatigue was probably the biggest one—I'd log in intending to engage deeply but end up just skimming because I was too tired to concentrate. Confusion made me avoid certain modules because tackling them felt insurmountable. Work responsibilities meant I'd sometimes go a week without meaningful LMS engagement. When everything piled up, I'd do the bare minimum on the platform and try to catch up with offline studying.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open, clicking through pages, maybe letting a video play, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about bills, thinking about family stuff, just zoning out from exhaustion. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times without absorbing anything. The engagement looked real on paper but I wasn't actually learning in those moments. It was performative studying, going through the motions because I felt I should be doing something.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly even though I knew participation mattered. The anxiety around putting my thoughts out there for others to see was paralyzing. I'd also avoid starting assignments until the last minute because the stress of potentially failing felt worse than the stress of rushing. Some readings I knew were important but I'd delay them because they seemed too dense to tackle when I was already overwhelmed. Avoidance became a coping mechanism, even though it made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due—logging in multiple times, frantically checking requirements, submitting at the last minute. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less because there was no external pressure cutting through my anxiety. The deadline panic was stressful but it was also the only thing that overcame my avoidance patterns.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But critical feedback sometimes sent me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid that type of work for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback because it helped me improve, but waiting for it was agonizing. Sometimes feedback came too late to actually help me adjust my approach for subsequent assignments.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having 18 online courses meant the LMS was a constant presence, but it also meant I could never fully focus on one thing. The modular structure helped me break things into chunks, but the sheer volume was crushing. I appreciated when courses had clear weekly expectations, but some were vague about priorities. The flexibility of online learning was essential for my situation, but it also meant I had to self-regulate everything, which is hard when you're anxious and stressed.
Q19: Clear, written instructions made engagement much easier—I could download them and refer back repeatedly. Courses that frontloaded all materials at the start were helpful because I could plan ahead. What made it harder was when content was released gradually and I'd fall behind, or when important information was only in videos I couldn't easily access. Overly complicated navigation in the LMS frustrated me when I was already short on time. Simple, text-heavy courses suited my learning style best.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. High activity might mean someone is desperately trying to understand material, searching for resources, re-watching videos, but still struggling to produce quality work. Or they might be dealing with anxiety that makes them check the LMS constantly without actually being productive. Sometimes you're engaged with the platform but too overwhelmed to complete assignments properly. Activity doesn't equal output when you're fighting against stress and limited resources.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me. My attendance was only about 14% and my assignment completion was around 50%, but I still got a Merit because I studied extensively offline and performed decently on exams. Someone looking at my LMS logs might think I was disengaged, but I was actually putting in 17 hours a week—just not in ways the system could see. Efficiency matters too; some students can learn deeply from minimal platform interaction.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work—the mechanical aspects of participation. They can track patterns like deadline-driven behavior or declining engagement over time. But they completely miss the quality of attention, offline studying, emotional state, and real comprehension. They can't capture the student crying at 2 AM trying to understand a concept, or the breakthrough that happens while reviewing printed notes on a bus. Logs show shadows of engagement, not the thing itself.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material—not just clicking through it. It's when I'm making connections, questioning ideas, applying concepts to my own life. True engagement involves emotional investment, even when that emotion is frustration. For me, engagement often happened away from the LMS, in quiet moments of genuine thinking. It's not about time spent or activities completed; it's about whether the learning is actually changing how I understand something.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to be here. It can't capture that I'm 28, working, supporting family, dealing with high anxiety, and still pushing through. It doesn't show the mindfulness exercises I did to manage my stress enough to study, or the tears, or the small victories. It can't measure resilience—how many times I wanted to quit but didn't. My engagement story is one of persistence against obstacles, and that narrative is completely invisible in login counts and completion percentages.
